title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text: A random variable is not a variable in the algebra sense and not really random — it’s a function that attaches a number to each outcome of an experiment. That small reframing is what turns probability into something you can average, add, and plot. Two species cover everything you need: discrete variables you can count, and continuous ones you can only measure.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Why it matters
Up to now we’ve talked about events — sets of outcomes. But almost every quantity a social scientist studies is a number: a test score, an income, a count of protests, a vote share. The random variable is the bridge: it lets us stop describing outcomes one set at a time and start summarising them with a single distribution, then compress that distribution into an expectation and a variance. Every histogram you’ll ever draw, every “the average effect was…”, every regression coefficient is downstream of this definition.
The discrete/continuous split also previews a practical fork: discrete data invite counts and proportions, continuous data invite densities and areas, and the two call for different tools throughout the book.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
What a random variable is
Formally, a random variable is a real-valued function defined on the sample space — it maps each outcome to a number (Ross 2018). The motivation is honest convenience: we are usually more interested in a function of the outcome than the raw outcome itself. Roll two dice and you rarely care about the ordered pair (4,2); you care about the sum, 6. “Sum of the dice” is a random variable (Ross 2018).
The two species:

Discrete — takes at most a countable set of values. The number of heads in ten flips, the count of yes-responses in a survey, the number of children in a household. You can list the possible values (Ross 2018).
Continuous — takes values in an uncountable continuum. A person’s exact weight, the lifetime of a device, a precise income or waiting time. You can measure it to arbitrary precision but never enumerate the possibilities (Ross 2018).

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
section: Describing the distribution: pmf, pdf, cdf
text:
Describing the distribution: pmf, pdf, cdf
How you assign probability depends on the species.
Discrete → probability mass function (pmf). The pmf gives the probability of each value, p(a) = P\{X = a\}, and these masses must sum to 1 over all possible values (Ross 2018). Picture a bar at each value whose height is its probability.
Continuous → probability density function (pdf). Here probability is an area. A density f(x) is a curve such that the probability X lands in a region B is the area under f over B (Ross 2018): 
P\{X \in B\} = \int_B f(x)\,dx, \qquad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1.







TipWhy P(X = x) = 0 for continuous variables



For a continuous variable, the probability of any exact value is zero — the “width” of a single point is zero, so the area above it is zero (Ross 2018). This sounds paradoxical (some value does occur!) but it’s the right bookkeeping: for continuous quantities, only intervals carry probability. “The chance someone weighs exactly 70.000… kg” is 0; “the chance they weigh between 69.5 and 70.5 kg” is positive. The density f(x) is not a probability — it’s probability per unit length, which is why it can exceed 1.


Both → cumulative distribution function (cdf). The cdf works for any random variable: 
F(b) = P\{X \le b\}.
 It accumulates probability from the left. For a discrete variable it’s a step function (jumping at each value by that value’s mass); for a continuous variable it’s a smooth curve, and the pdf is exactly its derivative, f(x) = F'(x) (Ross 2018). The cdf is the one description that unifies the two species — handy whenever you need “the probability of at most” or want to compare distributions on equal footing.









Discrete
Continuous




Building block
pmf p(a) = P\{X=a\}
pdf f(x) (a density, not a probability)


Probability of a point
can be positive
always 0


Probability of a set
\sum_{a \in B} p(a)
\int_B f(x)\,dx (an area)


cdf F(b)=P\{X\le b\}
step function
smooth; f = F'


Total
\sum p(a) = 1
\int f = 1

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading a density as a probability. f(x) can be greater than 1; it is probability per unit, and only becomes a probability once integrated over an interval. Heights of a pdf are not chances.
Asking for P(X = x) on continuous data. It’s always 0. Rephrase as an interval, or use the cdf for “at most”/“at least.”
Forcing continuous data into the discrete mold (or vice versa). Counts (protests, children) are genuinely discrete; binning them or modelling them as Normal can mislead, especially for rare counts (see the Poisson).
Forgetting the distribution must sum/integrate to 1. A proposed pmf or pdf that doesn’t normalise isn’t a valid distribution — a quick, error-catching sanity check.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — random variable as a real-valued function on the sample space (the “sum of two dice” motivation); discrete (countable) vs continuous (uncountable) variables; pmf and its summing to 1; pdf with P\{X\in B\}=\int_B f; P(X=x)=0 for continuous variables; the cdf F(b)=P\{X\le b\} as a step function (discrete) and the pdf as the derivative of the cdf (continuous).

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Name the random variable explicitly. Writing “Let X = number of yes-responses” forces you to fix the support and the species before computing anything.
Decide discrete vs continuous early. It selects your whole toolkit — masses vs densities, sums vs integrals, bar charts vs histograms.
Use the cdf for “at least / at most” questions. It sidesteps fiddly summation and is the natural object for quantiles and percentiles later.
Always check it normalises. If your masses don’t sum to 1 (or your density doesn’t integrate to 1), something is wrong before you’ve even started.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Properties of the cdf. For any random variable, F is (i) non-decreasing, (ii) right- continuous, with (iii) \lim_{b\to-\infty}F(b)=0 and \lim_{b\to\infty}F(b)=1. Probabilities of intervals come straight from it: 
P\{a < X \le b\} = F(b) - F(a).

Discrete cdf as a sum of jumps. F(b) = \sum_{a \le b} p(a); the jump at each value a is exactly p(a) = F(a) - F(a^-), recovering the pmf from the cdf.
Continuous cdf and pdf. F(b) = \int_{-\infty}^{b} f(x)\,dx, so by the fundamental theorem of calculus f(x) = F'(x) wherever F is differentiable. The probability of an exact point is P\{X=x\} = F(x) - F(x^-) = 0 because F is continuous.
Functions of a random variable. If Y = g(X), its distribution can be found from X’s — but remarkably, you don’t need Y’s distribution to get its mean: that’s the job of LOTUS in the next entry.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Connections

Builds on: Sample spaces & the axioms — a random variable is a function on the sample space, and its probabilities inherit the axioms.
Leads to: Expectation & variance — once you have a distribution, you summarise it by a centre and a spread.
Pays off in: the common distributions (named pmfs and pdfs) and the sampling distribution of a statistic, which is itself the distribution of a random variable.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Define a random variable as a function on the sample space using Ross’s ‘sum of two dice’ motivation, and distinguish discrete from continuous variables with examples.”
“Contrast the pmf, pdf, and cdf, and explain carefully why P(X=x)=0 for a continuous variable while the density can exceed 1.”
“Show how the cdf relates to the pmf (jumps) and to the pdf (derivative), and compute P\{a < X \le b\} from the cdf.”
“Give three social-science quantities and classify each as discrete or continuous, with the appropriate description (pmf or pdf).”

title: What Probability Actually Is
text: A probability is a number between 0 and 1, but the meaning of that number is contested. “The coin has probability ½ of heads” can mean three different things: a count of symmetric possibilities, a long-run frequency, or a degree of belief. The mathematics is identical across all three; the interpretation changes what kind of question you’re answering — and which research tradition you’re standing in.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
Why it matters
You will not get far in quantitative social science before all three readings show up. When a methods text says a fair die lands on each face with probability ⅙, that’s the classical reading. When a pollster says their estimator “is right 95% of the time,” that’s the frequentist reading — the one that underwrites confidence intervals and p-values. When a forecaster says a candidate has a “70% chance of winning” an election that happens exactly once, that’s the subjective reading — the one that underwrites Bayesian inference.
Beginners trip because they assume “probability” means one thing. It doesn’t. Knowing which interpretation is in play tells you what would count as evidence, and it is the quiet reason the frequentist and Bayesian chapters later in this book feel like different philosophies rather than different formulas. The good news, established at the end of this entry: whichever reading you adopt, the rules are the same.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
The three interpretations

1. Classical — counting equally likely outcomes
The oldest view: if a situation has a finite number of outcomes and symmetry makes them equally likely, then the probability of an event is just a ratio of counts (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997):

P(E) = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } E}{\text{number of outcomes in } S}.

This is “probability as careful counting.” Grinstead & Snell credit Gerolamo Cardano (a 16th-century gambler-mathematician) with the crucial first move — choosing the correct sample space for dice problems (Grinstead and Snell 1997).

Worked example (Ross). Roll two fair dice. There are 6 \times 6 = 36 equally likely outcomes. Exactly 6 of them sum to 7 — (1,6),(6,1),(2,5),(5,2),(3,4),(4,3) — so P(\text{sum}=7) = 6/36 = 1/6 (Ross 2018).

Where it shows up in social science: randomised assignment. When you flip a coin to decide who gets a treatment, the equally-likely model is literally true by construction — which is exactly why randomisation gives causal inference its clean footing (Part V).
Its limit: the classical view requires symmetry. It can tell you about fair dice and shuffled cards, but it is mute about “the probability it rains tomorrow” or “the probability this defendant is guilty” — situations with no obvious set of equally likely outcomes.


2. Frequentist — the long-run fraction
The frequentist reading defines probability operationally: P(E) is the fraction of times E occurs as the experiment is repeated a large number of times (Ross 2018). The coin is “fair” because, tossed forever, the proportion of heads settles to ½.

Worked example (Grinstead & Snell). If a drug is effective 30% of the time, we assign probability 0.3 that it works for the next patient. In a simulation of 10,000 coin tosses, they obtained 5,039 heads — close to the “true” 0.5, and closer the longer you run it (Grinstead and Snell 1997).







NoteA subtle point worth pausing on



Ross notes that defining probability as a limiting frequency is philosophically awkward — it quietly assumes the long-run fraction settles to a fixed number, which is a strong, unprovable premise if taken as a definition. The modern fix (next entry) is to start from a few simple axioms and then prove that averages stabilise. That theorem is the Law of Large Numbers, and it is why the frequentist picture is a result of the theory rather than its foundation (Ross 2018).


Where it shows up: essentially all of classical inference. A 95% confidence interval is a frequentist promise — the procedure covers the truth 95% of the time across hypothetical repetitions of the study. Hold that phrasing; Part II makes it precise.
Its limit: it needs the event to be repeatable, at least in principle. It strains on genuinely one-off events — a particular election, a specific war, this firm’s chance of default next year.


3. Subjective (Bayesian) — a degree of belief
The subjective reading interprets probability as a measure of one person’s confidence in a statement, given what they know (Ross 2018). It applies happily to one-off events because it isn’t about repetition at all — it’s about the strength of a belief.

Worked example (Ross). Someone may be 70% certain Shakespeare wrote Julius Caesar and 10% certain Marlowe did. Crucially, to stay coherent, they should then be 80% certain it was one of the two — the beliefs must obey the same arithmetic as any other probabilities (Ross 2018).

Where it shows up: Bayesian inference (the dedicated chapter built on Lambert), risk forecasting, expert elicitation, and any setting where you want to attach a probability to a hypothesis itself (“how likely is this theory, given the data?”) rather than to data given a hypothesis.
Its limit (and its strength): the number is personal — two reasonable people with different information can assign different probabilities to the same event. Bayesians treat that not as a bug but as honesty about the role of prior knowledge.






TipThe unifying move



These are three interpretations, not three theories. All three agree that probabilities must obey the same rules — they live in [0,1], the certain event has probability 1, and the probabilities of mutually exclusive events add. That shared rulebook is the set of axioms in the next entry. So you can switch interpretive hats freely: compute with the axioms, and interpret the answer according to the question you’re asking.

title: What Probability Actually Is
section: Where it all started — and a cautionary tale
text:
Where it all started — and a cautionary tale
Probability theory was born from gambling. Grinstead & Snell trace the field to a 17th-century correspondence between Blaise Pascal and Pierre de Fermat, prompted by betting puzzles from the Chevalier de Méré (Grinstead and Snell 1997).
One puzzle is a perfect parable for this whole book. De Méré reasoned by a sloppy proportionality: a single die shows a 6 with decent odds in 4 rolls, so — scaling up — a pair of dice should show a double-6 with decent odds in 4 \times 6 = 24 rolls. He bet on it and lost money. Pascal and Fermat showed by exact calculation that you actually need 25 rolls for that bet to favour you (Grinstead and Snell 1997).
The lesson isn’t the arithmetic; it’s that plausible intuition about chance is often wrong, and only careful counting (the next two entries) sets it right. Probability is, in large part, the discipline of distrusting your gut about uncertainty.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming “equally likely” without earning it. The classical formula is only valid when symmetry genuinely holds. Real survey responses, real people, real outcomes are rarely uniform — assuming they are is a silent modelling error.
Reading a frequentist statement as a belief. “95% confidence” is a property of the procedure over many repetitions, not “95% probability this particular interval contains the truth.” Conflating the two is the single most common misreading in applied statistics (Part II returns to it with force).
Demanding repetition where there is none. Insisting on a strict frequentist reading makes one-off probabilities (“chance of recession next year”) sound meaningless. The subjective reading exists precisely for these.
Trusting proportional intuition about odds. De Méré’s mistake in modern dress: “twice the rolls, twice the chance” almost never holds. Compute, don’t extrapolate.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the classical ratio formula and the two-dice example; the frequentist interpretation and why defining probability as a limiting frequency is awkward (resolved later by the Law of Large Numbers); the subjective interpretation and the Shakespeare/Marlowe coherence example.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — Cardano and the correct sample space; the drug-efficacy and 10,000-toss simulation framings of frequency; the Pascal–Fermat history and the Chevalier de Méré 24-vs-25 rolls cautionary tale.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Name your interpretation before you argue. Most “is this probability right?” disputes dissolve once both sides say whether they mean a frequency or a belief.
Use the classical model as a sanity check. Whenever symmetry genuinely holds (fair coins, random assignment, shuffled data), counting gives an exact answer to test simulations against.
Default to frequentist language in classical inference, Bayesian language for one-off risk. The two chapters ahead are not rivals so much as tools for different question shapes.
When intuition and calculation disagree about odds, calculation wins. That instinct — the De Méré lesson — is most of what separates a careful analyst from a confident one.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
Connections

Leads to: Sample spaces, events & the axioms — the shared rulebook that makes all three interpretations compute the same way.
Pays off in: Bayes’ theorem and the Bayesian-inference chapter (the subjective view formalised), and all of Part II · Inference (the frequentist view formalised).
Foreshadows: the Law of Large Numbers, which turns the frequentist intuition into a provable theorem.

title: What Probability Actually Is
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Compare the classical, frequentist, and subjective interpretations of probability as presented in Ross and Grinstead & Snell, with the specific example each uses for each.”
“Explain why Ross says defining probability as a long-run frequency is philosophically awkward, and how the axiomatic approach plus the Law of Large Numbers resolves it.”
“Tell the full story of the Chevalier de Méré problem and the Pascal–Fermat correspondence, including why 24 rolls is a losing bet but 25 is a winning one.”
“Give three more real-world situations and say which interpretation of probability fits each best, and why.”

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text: Everything in the Classical Toolkit treated the parameter as a fixed unknown and the data as random. Bayesian inference flips that on its head: the data are the fixed thing you actually saw, and your uncertainty about the parameter is what gets a probability distribution. From that single inversion follows a different — and to many, more natural — way of reasoning under uncertainty.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Why it matters
Ask a frequentist “what’s the probability this candidate wins the election?” and they squirm: an election happens once, it can’t be rerun infinitely, so a long-run frequency doesn’t apply. (Lambert 2018) Yet you want to talk about that probability — and so does every forecaster, doctor, and analyst facing a one-off decision. The Bayesian framework exists to make that talk rigorous. It also delivers the thing people wrongly believed a confidence interval gave them: the ability to say “there’s a 95% probability the truth lies in this range.”
This entry rebuilds inference from a different foundation. It is not a rejection of what came before — the two frameworks often agree numerically — but a second language for uncertainty that you should be able to speak fluently. It extends Bayes’ theorem, which we met for events, to its grown-up job: reasoning about parameters.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Two philosophies of probability
The whole split begins with a disagreement about what the word “probability” means. (Lambert 2018)

Frequentist (objective). A probability is the long-run frequency of an event over infinitely many identical repetitions. It is a fixed feature of the world, independent of any observer — which is why it struggles with one-off events that can’t be repeated. (Lambert 2018)
Bayesian (subjective). A probability is a degree of belief — a number from 0 (certain it won’t happen) to 1 (certain it will) that measures your state of knowledge. Because it describes what you know rather than a law of nature, it can be attached to anything: a coin, an election, a one-time hypothesis. (Lambert 2018)

Neither is “right.” They are different definitions, and which you adopt reshapes every downstream move.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
section: The crucial inversion: what’s fixed, what’s random
text:
The crucial inversion: what’s fixed, what’s random
The practical heart of the difference is what each school holds fixed and what it lets vary. (Lambert 2018)









The parameter \theta
The data




Frequentist
Fixed unknown constant
Random — your sample is one of many “fictitious samples” you might have drawn


Bayesian
Random — described by a probability distribution for your uncertainty
Fixed — the data are the one thing you actually witnessed



For the frequentist, the truth \theta is a fixed pin and the data jiggle around it; inference imagines re-running the study. For the Bayesian, the data are given — they already happened — and the only uncertainty left is about \theta, so \theta is what carries a distribution. (Lambert 2018) This is epistemic uncertainty: not randomness in the world, but the honest admission that you don’t know the value, expressed as a probability. Once you make that move, the rest is mechanical.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Bayes’ rule for parameters
Bayesian inference is a machine for inverting a model — turning “this cause would produce that data” into “this data implies that cause.” (Lambert 2018) The engine is Bayes’ theorem, written for a parameter:

\underbrace{p(\theta \mid \text{data})}_{\text{posterior}} \;\propto\;
\underbrace{p(\text{data} \mid \theta)}_{\text{likelihood}} \;\times\;
\underbrace{p(\theta)}_{\text{prior}} .

Lambert calls this the central dogma of Bayesian statistics, and each piece has a clean meaning: (Lambert 2018)

The prior p(\theta) — what you believe before seeing the data, as a full distribution over the parameter’s possible values, drawn from past experience or expert judgement.
The likelihood p(\text{data}\mid\theta) — what the data say: how probable your actual sample would be for each candidate value of \theta. Crucially, viewed as a function of \theta (with the data fixed), the likelihood is not a probability distribution — its area doesn’t integrate to 1. (Lambert 2018)
The posterior p(\theta\mid\text{data}) — the goal: your updated belief after folding in the data. It contains everything you now know about \theta, and its width is your uncertainty — a narrow posterior means high confidence. (Lambert 2018)

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
The posterior as a compromise
The most useful intuition Lambert offers: the posterior is a compromise — a weighted average — between the prior and the likelihood, a tug-of-war whose rope settles between the two pulls. (Lambert 2018) The posterior’s peak always lands between the prior’s peak and the likelihood’s peak.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import lgamma

def beta_pdf(theta, a, b):
    logB = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b)
    return np.exp((a - 1) * np.log(theta) + (b - 1) * np.log(1 - theta) - logB)

theta = np.linspace(1e-3, 1 - 1e-3, 500)
prior = beta_pdf(theta, 2, 5)      # skeptical: leans toward low bias, mode 0.2
like  = beta_pdf(theta, 8, 4)      # normalized likelihood for 7 heads in 10, mode 0.7
post  = beta_pdf(theta, 9, 8)      # Beta(2+7, 5+3): posterior, mode 0.53

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.plot(theta, prior, color="#9aa0a6", lw=2.0, label="Prior  Beta(2,5) — skeptical")
ax.plot(theta, like,  color="#d98a29", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="Likelihood — 7 heads in 10")
ax.plot(theta, post,  color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label="Posterior  Beta(9,8)")
for m, c in [(0.2, "#9aa0a6"), (0.7, "#d98a29"), (8/15, "#1f3a5f")]:
    ax.axvline(m, color=c, ls=":", lw=1.0, alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel("θ — the coin's bias  (probability of heads)")
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title("The posterior is a compromise between prior belief and data")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Bayesian updating as a tug-of-war. A skeptical prior (grey, leaning low) meets data of 7 heads in 10 flips (orange likelihood, pulling toward 0.7). The posterior (blue) settles in between — closer to the data here, but pulled down by the prior. With more flips the orange curve would sharpen and drag the blue one onto it.





The dotted lines mark the three peaks: prior at 0.2, likelihood at 0.7, posterior at 0.53 — squarely between. And the power of data is built into the picture: as the sample grows, the likelihood narrows and sharpens, until it swamps the prior and the posterior slides almost entirely onto the data. (Lambert 2018) A profound consequence follows: two researchers who start with different priors will converge on the same posterior once enough data arrives. (Lambert 2018) The prior matters most exactly when data is scarce — which is also when honestly declaring your assumptions matters most.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
section: Sequential updating: today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior
text:
Sequential updating: today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior
Because the posterior simply is your updated state of knowledge, it becomes the prior for the next batch of data. (Lambert 2018) Learning is recursive: update, then update again, each posterior feeding forward. Lambert proves a reassuring property — order invariance: you reach the identical final posterior whether you swallow all the data at once or digest it piece by piece. (Lambert 2018) The arithmetic of belief is consistent, however you slice the evidence.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Lambert’s gallery of intuitions
Lambert teaches the worldview through vivid scenes, several of which we already met for events in the Bayes’ theorem entry — now read them as inference about a parameter: (Lambert 2018)

The Bayesian Coastguard. A ship is lost in a storm. The search starts with a wide circular prior (the 25 km radio range). A faint signal — the likelihood — reshapes it into an oval posterior. A second tower’s reading (sequential updating) shrinks the search to a tiny high-probability patch, where the crew is found. Inference as a search that concentrates belief.
The Murder Trial. A frequentist juror asks only “how likely is this camera footage if the defendant were guilty?” and convicts if that’s small. A Bayesian juror folds in a prior — the defendant’s character, the existence of other suspects — and can rationally conclude only a 1 in 1,000 chance of guilt despite the footage, because the prior was so strongly exculpatory. The base rate is not a distraction; it is evidence.
The Penguin Tail. The cleanest contrast of all. A frequentist 95% confidence interval of 1%–5% red-tailed penguins describes a procedure that brackets the truth 95% of the time across infinite fictitious samples. A Bayesian 95% credible interval lets you say the thing you always wanted to: “there’s a 95% probability the true value is in this range.” Same headline number, fundamentally different — and more direct — meaning. (We build credible intervals properly in B3.)

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Thinking the prior is “cheating.” A prior is an explicit, auditable assumption — and with enough data it washes out. Frequentist methods make assumptions too (the model, the test); the Bayesian just writes one of them down as a distribution. (Lambert 2018)
Treating the likelihood as a probability distribution over \theta. It isn’t — as a function of \theta it need not integrate to 1. It is the weight the data assign to each parameter value, not a belief about them. (Lambert 2018)
Reporting a posterior without its width. The point of going Bayesian is the whole distribution. A posterior mean with no spread throws away the uncertainty you worked to quantify. (Lambert 2018)
Forgetting that priors dominate when data is thin. With small samples the prior drives the posterior, so a careless prior can mislead. Convergence is a large-sample promise — check prior sensitivity when n is small (the subject of B2). (Lambert 2018)
Confusing a credible interval with a confidence interval. They answer different questions. The credible interval is a statement about the parameter; the confidence interval is a statement about the procedure. (Lambert 2018)

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — the entire entry: the two philosophies of probability; the fixed-data / random-parameter inversion and epistemic uncertainty; the “central dogma” p(\theta\mid\text{data}) \propto p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta) and the meaning of prior, likelihood (and why it isn’t a distribution), and posterior; the posterior as a compromise / weighted average and data swamping the prior; sequential updating and order invariance; the Coastguard, Murder Trial, Fish Game, and Penguin Tail examples.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the frequentist long-run-frequency baseline against which the Bayesian view is contrasted.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Bayes’ theorem for a parameter. With a continuous parameter \theta,

p(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)}{p(\text{data})},
\qquad
p(\text{data}) = \int p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)\,d\theta .

The denominator p(\text{data}) — the evidence — does not depend on \theta; it is just the constant that makes the posterior integrate to 1. That is why we usually write the proportional form p(\theta\mid\text{data}) \propto p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta) and normalise at the end. (Computing that integral is the hard part — see B2 and B3.)
Why the figure works: the Beta–Binomial. For a coin with bias \theta, k heads in n flips has binomial likelihood p(\text{data}\mid\theta) \propto \theta^{k}(1-\theta)^{n-k}. With a \text{Beta}(a,b) prior, p(\theta) \propto \theta^{a-1}(1-\theta)^{b-1}, the posterior is

p(\theta\mid\text{data}) \propto \theta^{a+k-1}(1-\theta)^{b+n-k-1}
= \text{Beta}(a+k,\; b+n-k).

In the figure: prior \text{Beta}(2,5), data k=7 heads in n=10, posterior \text{Beta}(2+7,\,5+3)=\text{Beta}(9,8). The modes are (a-1)/(a+b-2): prior 1/5=0.2, likelihood 7/10=0.7, posterior 8/15\approx0.53 — the posterior mode lies between, as the compromise picture requires.
Order invariance. Updating on D_1 then D_2 gives p(\theta\mid D_1,D_2) \propto p(D_2\mid\theta)\,p(D_1\mid\theta)\,p(\theta), which is symmetric in D_1 and D_2 — so the final posterior is independent of the order, and identical to a single batch update on (D_1,D_2).

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Connections

Builds on: Bayes’ theorem (the same rule, now applied to parameters rather than events), random variables & distributions (the prior and posterior are distributions), and estimation (the Bayesian posterior vs the frequentist point estimate; MLE reappears as the likelihood’s peak).
Leads to: the machinery — priors, likelihoods, posteriors (how it’s actually computed) and credible intervals & computation (reading the posterior and the verdict on Bayesian vs frequentist).
Answers: the question the confidence interval couldn’t — a genuine probability statement about the parameter — and offers one response to the replication crisis’s abuse of p-values.

title: The Bayesian Worldview
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“Explain Lambert’s distinction between the frequentist and Bayesian definitions of probability, and why the Bayesian view handles one-off events more naturally.”
“Walk me through the Bayesian Coastguard analogy in full, and map each part onto prior, likelihood, posterior, and sequential updating.”
“Why does Lambert insist the likelihood is not a probability distribution? Explain carefully with an example.”
“Show me Lambert’s Murder Trial contrast between frequentist and Bayesian reasoning, including the 1-in-1,000 conclusion.”
“Explain how and why two analysts with different priors converge on the same posterior as data accumulates.”

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text: A posterior is the whole answer — but you still have to read it, and for any realistic model you can’t even write it down in closed form. This entry does both: it shows how to summarise a posterior into the interval people actually wanted, how a clever random walk (MCMC) conjures that posterior without the impossible integral, and where, in the end, Bayesian and frequentist thinking each earn their keep.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Why it matters
This is the last entry of Part II, and it closes two loops. First, it delivers the interval the confidence interval couldn’t — one you may legitimately read as “95% probability the parameter is in here.” Second, it confronts the wall raised in B2: the denominator is an intractable integral, so how does anyone do Bayesian inference on a real problem? The answer — simulation — is the engine behind the entire modern Bayesian toolkit, and once you grasp its core trick the rest is detail. Then we step back and weigh the two paradigms honestly, because a quantitative social scientist should be bilingual, not partisan.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
The credible interval
A credible interval is a range read straight off the posterior that contains a stated share of its probability mass — a 95% credible interval holds 95% of the posterior. (Lambert 2018) And here is the pay-off the whole sub-chapter has been building toward:






ImportantWhat a Bayesian may say



A frequentist cannot say “there is a 95% probability the true value lies in 1%–5%,” because to them the parameter is a fixed constant — the interval either contains it or doesn’t. A Bayesian can say exactly that, because the parameter is a random variable carrying a distribution. (Lambert 2018)


The credible interval describes post-data uncertainty about the specific parameter — your actual state of knowledge now — rather than the long-run behaviour of a procedure. (Lambert 2018) Lambert’s running example: a 95% credible interval of 0\% \le \text{RT} \le 4\% for the proportion of red-tailed penguins is a direct statement that the true proportion is, with 95% probability, in that range. (Lambert 2018) It is the interval everyone always thought they were getting.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Equal-tailed vs highest-posterior-density
There are two ways to carve a 95% region out of a posterior. (Lambert 2018)

Equal-tailed (central) interval — trim the bottom 2.5% and top 2.5% of the mass; the interval runs between the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles and is centred on the median.
Highest posterior density (HPD) interval — the set of values such that every point inside has higher posterior density than every point outside. It is the shortest region holding 95%, and for a multi-peaked posterior it can be non-contiguous.







TipLambert’s treasure-hunting pirate



A pirate hunts buried gold. A central interval forces him to dig one long contiguous stretch — say 1.5 km to 8.5 km of beach — even where the gold is unlikely in the middle. An HPD interval lets him dig only the truly promising patches — 1–3 km and 7–9 km — skipping the barren middle and saving enormous excavation cost. (Lambert 2018) When the posterior is single-peaked and symmetric the two intervals coincide; when it is skewed or bimodal, HPD is sharper.



Credible vs confidence, on the same data
Here is the contrast made concrete. We flip a coin 20 times, see 14 heads, and form both 95% intervals. The Bayesian posterior (flat prior) is \text{Beta}(15,7); its credible interval is shaded. The frequentist Wald interval sits just below. The numbers nearly coincide — but what they claim could not be more different.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import lgamma

def beta_pdf(theta, a, b):
    logB = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b)
    return np.exp((a - 1) * np.log(theta) + (b - 1) * np.log(1 - theta) - logB)

k, n = 14, 20
a_post, b_post = 1 + k, 1 + (n - k)          # flat prior Beta(1,1) -> Beta(15,7)
theta = np.linspace(1e-4, 1 - 1e-4, 2000)
post = beta_pdf(theta, a_post, b_post)

# 95% equal-tailed credible interval by numerical CDF
cdf = np.cumsum(post) * (theta[1] - theta[0])
cdf /= cdf[-1]
lo_c = theta[np.searchsorted(cdf, 0.025)]
hi_c = theta[np.searchsorted(cdf, 0.975)]

# Frequentist Wald 95% CI
phat = k / n
se = np.sqrt(phat * (1 - phat) / n)
lo_f, hi_f = phat - 1.96 * se, phat + 1.96 * se

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.9))
ax.plot(theta, post, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2)
ax.fill_between(theta, post, where=(theta >= lo_c) & (theta <= hi_c),
                color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.25)
ax.errorbar(phat, -0.25, xerr=[[phat - lo_f], [hi_f - phat]], fmt="o",
            color="#c0392b", capsize=4, lw=2)
ax.text(hi_c + 0.01, beta_pdf(0.7, a_post, b_post) * 0.6,
        f"credible: [{lo_c:.2f}, {hi_c:.2f}]\n95% prob. θ in here",
        color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8.5)
ax.text(hi_f + 0.01, -0.25, f"confidence: [{lo_f:.2f}, {hi_f:.2f}]\nprocedure covers 95%",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5, va="center")
ax.set_xlim(0.3, 1.0)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("θ — the coin's bias")
ax.set_title("Same data, two intervals, two meanings")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two 95% intervals for a coin’s bias from 14 heads in 20 flips. The blue curve is the Bayesian posterior Beta(15,7); the shaded band is its 95% credible interval — ‘95% probability θ is in here.’ The red bar is the frequentist Wald confidence interval — ‘95% of such intervals would cover the fixed θ.’ Nearly the same numbers; fundamentally different meanings.





The lesson of the picture: agreement on the numbers hides a chasm in meaning. The credible interval is a claim about the parameter; the confidence interval is a claim about the procedure. In this simple, flat-prior case they nearly match — but in small samples with real prior information, or in complex models, they diverge, and only one of them answers the question you actually asked.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Why we must compute
For all but textbook models, the posterior has no closed form, because the normalising denominator p(\text{data}) = \int p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)\,d\theta is an integral over every parameter value — and in a multi-parameter model that is high-dimensional. (Lambert 2018) Lambert’s recurring example: test scores across 100 schools can demand a 200-dimensional integral, which no computer can solve exactly or even approximate by brute force. (Lambert 2018) If we had to evaluate that denominator, applied Bayesian statistics would be dead in the water.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
MCMC: sampling without the integral
The escape is Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) — instead of computing the posterior, draw samples from it, and let the cloud of samples stand in for the distribution. (Lambert 2018) The magic is that you can do this without ever computing the denominator.
MCMC is a guided random walk: each new sample depends on the current one, and the walk is rigged so it lingers in regions of high posterior density. Over time the collected points mirror the posterior’s shape. (Lambert 2018) The simplest version is the Metropolis algorithm:

Propose a step to a nearby location.
If the posterior density there is higher (uphill), accept — always move toward more probable values.
If it is lower (downhill), accept only with probability r, the ratio of the new density to the old. So the walk usually climbs but occasionally wanders down, which is what lets it explore the whole distribution instead of getting stuck on the peak. (Lambert 2018)







TipLambert’s explorer in the fog



Imagine mapping a mountain range permanently shrouded in fog. You can’t photograph it from a satellite (you can’t see the whole posterior). But you can take local steps, recording your altitude and coordinates at each. Take enough steps — preferring uphill but sometimes slipping down — and your notebook reconstructs a full contour map of the range. That notebook is your set of MCMC samples. (Lambert 2018)


The crucial sleight of hand lives in step 3: because r is a ratio of two posterior densities, the intractable denominator p(\text{data}) — identical top and bottom — cancels exactly. MCMC needs only the unnormalised product likelihood × prior, which we can always evaluate. (The algebra is in the appendix.)

Watch a Metropolis sampler work
Below, a Metropolis sampler — knowing only the unnormalised target \theta^{14}(1-\theta)^6, never the normaliser — recovers the \text{Beta}(15,7) posterior from the figure above. The left panel is the trace (the walk’s path over time); the right is the histogram of its samples with the true posterior overlaid. The simulation never computed an integral.


Show the sampler code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import lgamma

rng = np.random.default_rng(20)

def log_target(t):                      # log of theta^14 (1-theta)^6, up to a constant
    if t <= 0 or t >= 1:
        return -np.inf
    return 14 * np.log(t) + 6 * np.log(1 - t)

n_steps, burn = 8000, 2000
samples = np.empty(n_steps)
t = 0.5
lt = log_target(t)
for i in range(n_steps):
    prop = t + rng.normal(0, 0.08)      # propose a nearby step
    lp = log_target(prop)
    if np.log(rng.random()) < lp - lt:  # accept uphill always, downhill with prob r
        t, lt = prop, lp
    samples[i] = t

post = samples[burn:]

def beta_pdf(theta, a, b):
    logB = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b)
    return np.exp((a - 1) * np.log(theta) + (b - 1) * np.log(1 - theta) - logB)

fig, (axT, axH) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.4),
                               gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.5, 1]})
axT.plot(np.arange(burn), samples[:burn], color="#9aa0a6", lw=0.5)
axT.plot(np.arange(burn, n_steps), post, color="#1f3a5f", lw=0.5)
axT.axvline(burn, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.0)
axT.text(burn + 100, 0.92, "burn-in discarded", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axT.set_title("Trace: the 'fuzzy caterpillar'", fontsize=10)
axT.set_xlabel("step"); axT.set_ylabel("θ"); axT.set_ylim(0.3, 1.0)

xs = np.linspace(0.3, 1.0, 300)
axH.hist(post, bins=40, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.45, orientation="horizontal")
axH.plot(beta_pdf(xs, 15, 7), xs, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.0)
axH.set_title(f"Samples vs Beta(15,7)\nmean ≈ {post.mean():.3f}", fontsize=10)
axH.set_ylim(0.3, 1.0); axH.set_yticks([]); axH.set_xlabel("density")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: A Metropolis sampler recovering Beta(15,7) using only the unnormalised target. Left: the trace plot — a healthy ‘fuzzy caterpillar’ oscillating around a stable centre after a short burn-in (grey). Right: the histogram of post-burn-in samples matches the analytic posterior (blue curve) — the denominator was never computed.





The sampler’s mean \approx 0.68 recovers the analytic posterior mean 15/22 = 0.682 — and it did so by walking, never by integrating. Scale this idea up (with smarter proposals like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) and it handles the 200-dimensional models that defeat pen and paper.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Summarising with samples
Once MCMC hands you thousands of draws, the Law of Large Numbers does the rest — sample summaries approximate posterior summaries. (Lambert 2018)

Posterior mean ≈ the average of the samples.
95% credible interval ≈ the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the sorted samples — no formula, just counting. (Lambert 2018)
Posterior variance ≈ the variance of the samples.

The posterior you couldn’t write down becomes a list of numbers you can summarise any way you like.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
section: Diagnostics: did the walk actually work?
text:
Diagnostics: did the walk actually work?
Because MCMC starts somewhere arbitrary and explores blindly, you must check that it found the real posterior. (Lambert 2018)

Burn-in (warm-up). Early samples are contaminated by the random starting point, so discard them — often the first half of the chain — keeping only draws from the settled distribution. (Lambert 2018)
Trace plots. Plot the walk over time. A healthy chain is a “fuzzy caterpillar” — rapidly oscillating around a stable centre (the left panel above). A trace that drifts or sticks is a warning. (Lambert 2018)
Multiple chains — “Bob’s Bees.” Release one bee in a dark house and it may get stuck in a single room, fooling you into thinking it mapped the whole place. Release several bees from different starting points; if they all converge on the same regions, you can trust the map. (Lambert 2018)
\hat R (R-hat). The quantitative version of Bob’s Bees: it compares variation within chains to variation between chains. Convergence is signalled by \hat R \approx 1.0; values much above 1 mean the chains disagree and haven’t converged. (Lambert 2018)

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
The verdict: Bayesian vs frequentist
Lambert’s closing stance is refreshingly even-handed — the two schools are different paths to the same goal of learning from noisy data. (Lambert 2018)
Where Bayesian wins:

Natural uncertainty statements. “95% probability the effect is positive” is what policymakers actually want, and it is a direct Bayesian output. (Lambert 2018)
Prior information. Past studies and expert knowledge enter the model formally rather than informally. (Lambert 2018)
Small samples. Bayesian inference stays coherent when frequentist asymptotic (large-n) approximations break down. (Lambert 2018)
Flexibility. MCMC fits models of essentially arbitrary complexity — hierarchical, multilevel, custom — that have no closed-form frequentist solution. (Lambert 2018)

Where critics push back:

Subjectivity. Choosing a prior strikes some as unscientific — though Lambert’s retort stands: frequentist model and test choices are equally subjective, just less visible. (Lambert 2018)
Computational cost. MCMC can be slow and demanding for big models. (Lambert 2018)







TipLambert’s practical guidance: don’t be a zealot



Start simple — frequentist OLS or maximum likelihood to get an initial feel for the data — then build up to Bayesian models as complexity and the need for rigorous uncertainty quantification grow. And whatever you fit, treat posterior predictive checks (does data simulated from your fitted model resemble the real data?) as your “best friend.” (Lambert 2018)


That is the right note to end Part II on: the tools are not tribes to join but instruments to choose between, by the job in front of you.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Calling a confidence interval a credible interval. Only the Bayesian interval licenses “95% probability the parameter is in here.” Don’t smuggle that reading into a frequentist CI. (Lambert 2018)
Trusting un-diagnosed MCMC. A pretty number from a chain that never converged is fiction. Always check trace plots, run multiple chains, and confirm \hat R \approx 1. (Lambert 2018)
Forgetting burn-in. Including the arbitrary starting transient biases every summary. Discard the warm-up. (Lambert 2018)
Using a central interval on a skewed or bimodal posterior. It can include low-density regions and exclude high-density ones; reach for HPD when the shape isn’t symmetric. (Lambert 2018)
Becoming a zealot — either way. Bayesian isn’t automatically “better.” Match the method to the problem; start frequentist, escalate to Bayesian when the question demands it. (Lambert 2018)

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — the whole entry: the credible interval and the “95% probability” contrast with the confidence interval (penguin example); equal-tailed vs HPD and the treasure-hunting pirate; the intractable denominator and the 200-dimensional integral; MCMC as a guided random walk, the Metropolis accept/reject rule, and the explorer-in-the-fog analogy; summarising the posterior from samples via the Law of Large Numbers; burn-in, the fuzzy-caterpillar trace, Bob’s Bees / multiple chains, and \hat R \approx 1; the even-handed Bayesian-vs-frequentist verdict and the “start simple, don’t be a zealot, use posterior predictive checks” guidance.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the Law of Large Numbers that justifies approximating posterior quantities by sample averages.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Bayesian machinery (the intractable denominator that forces sampling), the Bayesian worldview (the credible interval as the payoff of treating the parameter as random), confidence intervals (the frequentist foil), and the Law of Large Numbers (why sample summaries approximate the posterior).
Closes: Part II. You now hold both inferential languages — the Classical Toolkit and the Bayesian alternative — and can choose between them by the problem, not by tribe.
Leads to: Bayesian and frequentist regression in Part III, the hierarchical and regularised models of Part IV (where MCMC and weakly-informative priors are standard), and the uncertainty quantification behind every causal estimate.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“Explain Lambert’s distinction between a credible interval and a confidence interval, and exactly what each one licenses you to say.”
“Walk me through the equal-tailed versus highest-posterior-density interval using the treasure-hunting analogy.”
“Explain the Metropolis algorithm step by step and the explorer-in-the-fog analogy, and show why the denominator cancels.”

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text: A hypothesis test asks one disciplined question: if nothing were going on, how surprising would my data be? The p-value measures that surprise. It is a genuinely useful number wrapped in a thick fog of misunderstanding — and clearing the fog is most of what it takes to use it well.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Why it matters
Null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) is the most widely used statistical procedure in all of science. (Bruce et al. 2020) It is also the most widely abused, and the gap between those two facts is the engine of the replication crisis. The trouble is rarely the arithmetic; it is that the p-value answers a narrow, conditional question while researchers crave a broad, unconditional one — and the slippage between the two has corrupted whole literatures.
So this entry does two things at once: it builds the testing machinery honestly, and it polices the single most consequential misreading in quantitative research. Get the definition of a p-value exactly right and most of the downstream sins become visible.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The logic: proof by contradiction
A hypothesis test is an argument by contradiction. (Bruce et al. 2020) You set up two rival claims:

The null hypothesis H_0 — the “nothing special is happening” baseline: no effect, no difference, no relationship. It exists to be falsified. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
The alternative hypothesis H_1 — the research hypothesis, the effect you suspect is real.

You then assume the null is true and ask whether the data you actually collected is wildly inconsistent with that assumption. If the data would be bizarrely unlikely under “no effect,” you reject the null and take that as evidence for the alternative. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) The asymmetry is deliberate and important: you never prove the null, you only fail to reject it — exactly as a court returns “not guilty,” not “innocent.”

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
section: The test statistic: a surprise meter
text:
The test statistic: a surprise meter
How do you measure “inconsistent with the null”? You compress the data into a single test statistic — one number summarising how far your result sits from what the null predicts. (Bruce et al. 2020) Think of it as a surprise meter: the larger its magnitude, the more your data departs from the null’s expectation, and the more the null looks like the wrong model for your world. (Bruce et al. 2020) A test statistic of 0.3 says “business as usual”; a 4.0 says “something is off.”

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The p-value, defined exactly
Here is the definition, and every word earns its place:

The p-value is the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. (Bruce et al. 2020)

It is the tail area — the probability that pure chance, with no real effect operating, would produce a result as striking as yours or more so. A small p-value means your data sits far out in the tail where the null rarely ventures, so the null becomes hard to believe.

The p-value as an area
That “tail area” is literal. Below is the sampling distribution of the test statistic under the null — the bell of results you’d see if nothing were going on. The observed statistic lands at \pm 2.1, and the p-value is simply the shaded area beyond it in both tails.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

nd = NormalDist()
z_obs = 2.1
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 600)
y = np.array([nd.pdf(v) for v in x])
p_two = 2 * (1 - nd.cdf(z_obs))   # two-tailed p-value ≈ 0.0357

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
ax.plot(x, y, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.8)
ax.fill_between(x, y, where=(x >=  z_obs), color="#c0392b", alpha=0.75)
ax.fill_between(x, y, where=(x <= -z_obs), color="#c0392b", alpha=0.75)
ax.axvline( z_obs, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls="--")
ax.axvline(-z_obs, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls="--")
ax.annotate(f"observed = +2.1", xy=(z_obs, nd.pdf(z_obs)), xytext=(2.4, 0.22),
            fontsize=9, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))
ax.set_title(f"p-value = shaded tail area ≈ {p_two:.3f}")
ax.set_xlabel("Test statistic (standardized), under the null")
ax.set_yticks([])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The p-value is a tail area. Under the null, the test statistic follows the bell shown. An observed value of 2.1 (two-tailed) cuts off the shaded tails — their combined area, here about 0.036, is the p-value: the chance of a result this extreme if nothing were going on.





If that shaded area is smaller than your pre-set threshold, you reject the null. The picture also makes the definition’s conditional vivid: the entire bell is drawn assuming the null is true. The p-value lives inside that assumption — which is exactly why it cannot, by itself, tell you whether the assumption holds.


What the p-value is not
This is the most important list in Part II. The p-value is: (Reinhart 2015)

NOT the probability that the null is true. It is computed assuming the null; it cannot turn around and report the null’s probability. (\Pr(\text{data}\mid H_0) is not \Pr(H_0\mid\text{data}) — flipping them is the transposed-conditional error, the same blunder as the base-rate fallacy.) (Reinhart 2015)
NOT the probability your result was a fluke. Same transposition: it gives the odds of the data if the null holds, not the odds favouring the null given the data. (Reinhart 2015)
NOT a measure of effect size. A microscopic, meaningless effect can yield p<0.001 if n is huge. Small p \ne big effect. (Bruce et al. 2020)
NOT the probability of replication, and a non-significant p>0.05 does NOT mean “no effect” — it may just mean the study was underpowered to detect one. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
section: The decision rule: α and the two errors
text:
The decision rule: α and the two errors
To turn the continuous p-value into a decision, you fix a threshold in advance — the significance level \alpha, conventionally 0.05 in social science. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) \alpha is a pre-committed error budget: it is the rate at which you are willing to reject a true null and cry wolf. Choosing it before seeing the data is what keeps the procedure honest.
Because the verdict is probabilistic, two mistakes are possible: (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)









H_0 true
H_0 false




Reject H_0
Type I error (false positive), rate \alpha
Correct — power = 1-\beta


Fail to reject
Correct
Type II error (false negative), rate \beta



A Type I error cries wolf — declaring a drug works when it doesn’t. A Type II error misses a real wolf — failing to notice a drug that genuinely works. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) They trade off: tightening \alpha to 0.01 cuts false positives but raises the false-negative rate \beta, costing you power — the subject of the next entry. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
One-tailed vs two-tailed
A two-tailed test asks “is there an effect in either direction?” and splits the rejection region across both tails (the case drawn above). A one-tailed test stakes everything on a direction chosen in advance (“B beats A”) and puts the whole rejection region in one tail, making it easier to reach significance there. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) The catch is non-negotiable: you must choose the direction before seeing the data. Switching to a one-tailed test after peeking, because it drags you under p<0.05, is a textbook form of p-hacking. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The workhorses: z- and t-tests
The z-test applies when the population standard deviation \sigma is known — rare outside standardized-test norming. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The t-test is what you actually use: \sigma is unknown, so you estimate it with the sample s, and the statistic follows Student’s t-distribution with its uncertainty-absorbing fat tails. (Bruce et al. 2020) The one-sample form is

t = \frac{\bar y - \mu_0}{\operatorname{se}(\bar y)} = \frac{\bar y - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt n},

the observed gap measured in standard errors. It comes in three flavours: (Bruce et al. 2020)

One-sample — compare a sample mean to a fixed benchmark. Does this class’s mean score differ from the district average of 105?
Two-sample (independent) — compare the means of two separate groups, with  t = \frac{\bar X_1 - \bar X_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}}.  Did the treatment group outscore the control group?
Paired (dependent) — compare the same units at two times, testing the mean of the differences. Did participants’ weight change from pre-test to post-test? Pairing strips out person-to-person variation and is far more powerful when it applies.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Statistical vs practical significance
The deepest trap in the whole apparatus: a result can be statistically significant yet practically trivial. (Bruce et al. 2020) Statistical significance is a binary yes/no about whether p<\alpha; practical (substantive) significance is about the magnitude and real-world importance of the effect. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) Wooldridge’s example: a study of baseball salaries might find a statistically significant effect of “runs per year” (p<0.05), but if each extra run is worth only $10, the effect is economically meaningless. (Wooldridge 2019) With a large enough n, everything becomes significant; the question that matters is how big, not whether. Always report and interpret the effect size, not just the verdict.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The duality with confidence intervals
Testing and interval estimation are the same logic in two costumes. (Wooldridge 2019) The bridge:

If the null value falls outside the 95% confidence interval, you reject H_0 at the 5% level.

So the scrap-rate interval [-2.28,\,-0.02] — which excludes zero — is a rejection of “no effect” at 5%. (Wooldridge 2019) The interval is strictly more informative than the test: it reports the whole range of effect sizes you can’t reject, not just a pass/fail. This is why a growing chorus urges reporting intervals and effect sizes over bare p-values — a reform we take up in the replication-crisis entry.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Reading p as “the probability the null is true” (or “the probability it’s a fluke”). The cardinal error — a transposed conditional. The p-value lives entirely inside the assumption that the null holds. (Reinhart 2015)
Reading a big p-value as proof of no effect. p>0.05 can mean “no effect” or “too little data to see one.” Absence of evidence isn’t evidence of absence — check the power. (Reinhart 2015)
Confusing statistical with practical significance. A trivially small effect is “significant” at large n; a large, important effect can be “non-significant” at small n. The verdict is not the magnitude. (Bruce et al. 2020; Wooldridge 2019)
Choosing one- vs two-tailed after seeing the data, or nudging \alpha to fit the result. Both are p-hacking; both inflate false positives far above the nominal 5%. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating p = 0.049 and p = 0.051 as categorically different. The 0.05 line is a convention, not a law of nature. A p-value is continuous evidence, not a cliff edge. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — NHST as proof by contradiction; the test statistic as a surprise meter; the exact p-value definition; small-p-from-large-n; z- vs t-tests and the three t-test forms.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — the catalogue of what a p-value is not (transposed conditional; not effect size; not replication probability); non-significance ≠ no effect; one- vs two-tailed switching and threshold-fiddling as p-hacking; the arbitrariness of 0.05.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — H_0/H_1 and the falsification logic; \alpha as a pre-committed error rate; Type I/II errors and their trade-off; statistical vs practical significance.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the baseball-salary example of statistical-but-not- economic significance; the CI–test duality and the scrap-rate interval.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

The one-sample t-test. Under H_0: \mu = \mu_0, with X_i i.i.d. and \sigma estimated by s, the statistic

T = \frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt n}

follows a t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom (exactly, if the X_i are Normal; approximately otherwise, by the CLT). The two-sided p-value is p = 2\,\Pr(t_{n-1} \ge |T_\text{obs}|).
Two-sample (Welch) form. For independent groups with possibly unequal variances,

T = \frac{\bar X_1 - \bar X_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}},

with Welch–Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. The denominator is the standard error of the difference — which is why two overlapping single-group intervals can still yield a significant T: the SE of a difference is not the sum of the two separate SEs (see the overlap fallacy).
Paired form. Reduce to one sample by differencing: let D_i = X_i^{\text{post}} -
X_i^{\text{pre}} and run a one-sample test of \bar D against 0. Because \operatorname{Var}(D) =
\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\operatorname{Cov}(X^{\text{pre}}, X^{\text{post}}) and the within-person correlation is usually positive, pairing shrinks the standard error — the source of its extra power.
Why the CI–test duality holds. The 95% interval is exactly the set of null values \mu_0 that would not be rejected at the 5% level: |{\bar X - \mu_0}|/(s/\sqrt n) \le t_{n-1,0.975} rearranges to \mu_0 \in [\bar X \pm t_{n-1,0.975}\, s/\sqrt n]. Reject \iff \mu_0 outside the interval.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Connections

Builds on: confidence intervals (its dual), estimation (the standard error in the denominator), sampling distributions & the CLT (the null distribution of the statistic), and the base-rate fallacy (the transposed conditional that wrecks p-value interpretation).
Leads to: statistical power (the Type II / \beta side of the table) and the replication crisis (what happens when testing is abused at scale).
Reappears as: the t- and F-statistics on every regression coefficient, and the significance tests behind every causal estimate.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
