title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text: The replication crisis is not a story about fraud. It is what happens when honest researchers, pushed by real incentives, use the tools of the previous six entries slightly wrong — flexibly, selectively, repeatedly — and a publication system rewards exactly that. The mathematics of why the literature fills with false positives is the natural capstone of this whole sub-chapter.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Why it matters
Across psychology, medicine, and economics, findings that everyone “knew” to be true have collapsed when independent labs tried to reproduce them. (Reinhart 2015) The unsettling part is the diagnosis: this is driven less by fraud than by the systemic misuse of statistical tools and the misaligned incentives of publishing. (Reinhart 2015) Every mechanism below is something a well-meaning researcher can do without ever feeling dishonest — which is precisely what makes it dangerous, and what makes understanding it a core competence rather than an ethics footnote.
This entry gathers the threads of Part II — p-values, power, confidence intervals, and the base-rate logic of Bayes — into one rigorous account of how science goes wrong at scale, and how it is being fixed.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: Researcher degrees of freedom: the garden of forking paths
text:
Researcher degrees of freedom: the garden of forking paths
Hand the same dataset to the same question, and a researcher still faces a thicket of small, individually defensible choices: which outliers to drop, which covariates to control for, how to code a borderline response, where to bin a continuous variable. Gelman and Loken named the result the garden of forking paths. (Reinhart 2015) Each fork looks reasonable in isolation; taken together they grant enormous researcher degrees of freedom — enough latitude to steer an analysis, even unconsciously, toward whatever comes out significant. Simulations show that flexible outlier handling and covariate selection alone can swing an effect size by a factor of two — routinely enough to drag a null result across the p<0.05 line. (Reinhart 2015)
The deep point: you do not need to cheat to exploit the forking paths. You only need to make each choice after glancing at the data and pick, at each fork, the path that looks a little better.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: P-hacking: torture the data until it confesses
text:
P-hacking: torture the data until it confesses
When that flexibility is turned, deliberately or not, toward chasing significance, it has a name: p-hacking (or data dredging) — running analysis after analysis until a p-value drops below 0.05, then reporting only the winners. (Bruce et al. 2020) The folk wisdom is exact: “torture the data long enough, and it will confess.” (Reinhart 2015)






NoteProving the Beatles make you younger



In a deliberately outlandish demonstration, researchers “showed” that listening to the Beatles’ When I’m Sixty-Four made participants literally younger in chronological age. They got there purely through flexible analysis — trying combinations of covariates and outcome variables until a significant result surfaced. The absurd conclusion was the point: the same freedom can manufacture a plausible-sounding effect just as easily, and you’d never know. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Optional stopping: the peeking problem
A special, seductive form of p-hacking concerns when you stop collecting data. Optional stopping means watching results arrive and halting the moment significance appears. (Reinhart 2015) It feels rational — why gather more once you’ve “found it”? — but it quietly wrecks the error rate. The \alpha=0.05 guarantee assumes a single test at a fixed sample size; peek after every participant and you take many bites at the apple, so the chance of eventually crossing p<0.05 by pure luck climbs far above 5%. (Reinhart 2015) Combine flexible analyses and flexible stopping and simulations put the false-positive rate above 50% for a single dataset — worse than a coin flip. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Multiple comparisons: the jelly-bean problem
The cleanest way to see false positives accumulate is to test many things at once. Each test at \alpha=0.05 has a 1-in-20 chance of a false alarm, so testing twenty near-guarantees one.






TipThe green jelly beans



Reinhart’s parable: test whether jelly beans cause acne and find nothing. So you test each colour — 20 of them. Nineteen show nothing; the green one hits p<0.05. Headline: “Green Jelly Beans Linked to Acne!” But you ran 20 tests; finding one false positive at the 5% level is exactly what chance predicts. (Reinhart 2015)


The arithmetic is brutal. For n independent tests, the probability of at least one false positive is

P(\text{at least one false positive}) = 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{n},

which reaches 99% by n = 100. (Reinhart 2015) And n runs into the thousands more often than you’d think — most memorably when researchers slid a dead Atlantic salmon into an fMRI scanner, showed it photographs, and ran thousands of uncorrected per-voxel tests. They found “significant” brain activity. In a dead fish. (Reinhart 2015)
The fixes adjust for the number of tests:

Bonferroni correction — divide \alpha by the number of tests (0.05/n). Simple and strict; it controls the chance of any false positive but costs power. (Reinhart 2015)
False discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg) — controls the proportion of significant results that are false alarms rather than the chance of any, retaining far more power when you expect some real effects among many tests. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Publication bias: the file drawer
Even flawless individual studies produce a broken literature if which studies get published depends on their results. Publication bias is journals’ preference for “exciting,” significant findings; the null results get filed away. (Reinhart 2015) So the published record is a biased sample of all studies run — selection bias, returned from the sampling entry to haunt science itself. If 20 labs run the same true-null study, the one that gets lucky at p<0.05 is the one the world sees; the 19 nulls vanish.






NoteWhen the file drawer was opened



When the suppressed trial data for the antidepressant reboxetine was finally unearthed, the “highly significant” benefit from the published studies evaporated: across all trials, the drug was no better than placebo. The published subset had told a confident, and false, story. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: Why most published findings can be false
text:
Why most published findings can be false
Put p-hacking, low power, and publication bias together and you get John Ioannidis’s famous claim, and it is just the base-rate fallacy wearing a lab coat. The chance that a significant finding is true — its positive predictive value (PPV) — depends on three things: the prior probability the hypothesis is true, the study’s power, and \alpha. (Reinhart 2015)






ImportantThe base-rate fallacy, in science



Suppose a field tests 100 hypotheses of which only 10 are true (a 10% base rate). With 80% power and \alpha=0.05:

True positives: 0.80 \times 10 = 8.
False positives: 0.05 \times 90 \approx 5.

So of the 13 “significant” findings, 5 — nearly 40% — are flukes. (Reinhart 2015) And that’s the optimistic case: drop power, add p-hacking, and the false share climbs past half.


This is the exact structure of the rare-disease Bayes calculation: a test can be individually reliable yet mostly wrong when the thing it screens for is rare. Significant findings are no different.

Watch the predictive value collapse
The PPV is governed by one formula, and plotting it is sobering. As the prior probability of true hypotheses falls — as a field reaches for more surprising, less likely effects — the share of “discoveries” that are real drops, fast, and low power drops it faster.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

alpha = 0.05
prior = np.linspace(0.005, 0.6, 400)

def ppv(power):
    tp = power * prior
    fp = alpha * (1 - prior)
    return tp / (tp + fp)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.plot(prior, ppv(0.80), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.0, label="80% power")
ax.plot(prior, ppv(0.35), color="#d98a29", lw=2.0, label="35% power (underpowered field)")
ax.axhline(0.5, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
ax.text(0.45, 0.52, "coin flip", color="#9aa0a6", fontsize=8)

# Ioannidis's 10% base-rate example at 80% power
p0 = 0.80 * 0.10 / (0.80 * 0.10 + alpha * 0.90)
ax.scatter([0.10], [p0], color="#c0392b", zorder=5)
ax.annotate(f"10% base rate → PPV ≈ {p0:.2f}", xy=(0.10, p0), xytext=(0.16, 0.45),
            fontsize=9, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))

ax.set_xlabel("Base rate: fraction of tested hypotheses that are true")
ax.set_ylabel("P(finding is true | significant)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title("Most 'discoveries' are false when the truth is rare")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The probability a significant finding is actually true (PPV), as a function of how often the tested hypotheses are true (the base rate), at α = 0.05. Even at 80% power, a field chasing unlikely hypotheses (low base rate) sees most of its ‘discoveries’ turn out false. Low power (35%) pushes the crossover further right. The dot marks Ioannidis’s 10%-base-rate example: PPV ≈ 0.64.





The lesson is structural, not moral: a field that prizes surprising results (low base rate) and tolerates underpowered studies (low power) will publish mostly false positives even if every individual researcher is honest. Reform has to change the system, not just scold the scientists.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
HARKing: the story told backwards
One more degree of freedom deserves its own name. HARKing — Hypothesizing After the Results are Known — is presenting a hypothesis cooked up after seeing the data as though it had been predicted in advance. (Reinhart 2015) It weaponises a deep human reflex: our talent for spinning a plausible story around any pattern that catches the eye. The fingerprint is exploratory research dressed as confirmatory — and it dissolves the entire logic of the p-value, which only means anything when the hypothesis is fixed before the data.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
The fixes: a new rigour
The encouraging half of the story is that the social sciences are responding, and the reforms map directly onto the diseases above: (Reinhart 2015; Bruce et al. 2020)

Pre-registration and registered reports — commit to the hypothesis and the full analysis plan before seeing the data; with registered reports the journal accepts the study on its design, before the results exist. This closes the garden of forking paths, p-hacking, optional stopping, and HARKing in one stroke, and defangs publication bias by deciding to publish before the result is known. (Reinhart 2015)
Replication — independent teams rerun studies; a finding earns trust by holding up, not by appearing once. (Reinhart 2015)
The ASA statement on p-values — the American Statistical Association’s six principles, formally warning that a p-value is not the probability a hypothesis is true and not a measure of an effect’s importance — the misreadings from the testing entry, now official. (Reinhart 2015)
Estimation over testing — report effect sizes and confidence intervals instead of bare significance verdicts, so readers see how big and how certain, not just whether. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Believing only fraudsters p-hack. The forking paths are walked by honest people making defensible choices. Good intentions are no protection; only pre-committed procedures are. (Reinhart 2015)
Running many tests and celebrating the one that’s significant. With 20 tests, one false positive is expected. Correct for multiplicity (Bonferroni or FDR) or pre-specify your primary outcome. (Reinhart 2015)
Trusting a literature of significant results. Publication bias means the published record overstates effects and hides the nulls. A single significant paper is weak evidence; a registered replication is strong. (Reinhart 2015)
Forgetting the base rate. A significant result for a surprising hypothesis is more likely a fluke than a discovery — exactly the Bayesian point. (Reinhart 2015)
Stopping data collection when it “looks significant.” Fix your sample size in advance (via a power analysis) or use a method designed for sequential peeking. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — researcher degrees of freedom and the garden of forking paths (Gelman & Loken); p-hacking and the Beatles example; optional stopping and the >50% false-positive simulations; the jelly-bean parable, the 1-(1-0.05)^n formula, and the dead salmon; Bonferroni; publication bias, the file drawer, and reboxetine; the PPV / Ioannidis base-rate argument; HARKing; pre-registration, replication, the ASA statement, and estimation over testing.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — p-hacking / data dredging; the false discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg) as a higher-power alternative to Bonferroni; the push toward effect sizes and intervals.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Family-wise error from multiple tests. For m independent true nulls each tested at level \alpha, the probability of no false positive is (1-\alpha)^m, so the family-wise error rate is

\text{FWER} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^m .

At \alpha = 0.05: m=10 \Rightarrow 0.40; m=100 \Rightarrow 0.99. Bonferroni tests each at \alpha/m, since 1-(1-\alpha/m)^m \le \alpha keeps the FWER at or below \alpha.
Positive predictive value. Let \pi be the prior probability a tested hypothesis is true, 1-\beta the power, \alpha the level. Among many tests,

\text{PPV} = \Pr(\text{true}\mid \text{significant})
= \frac{(1-\beta)\,\pi}{(1-\beta)\,\pi + \alpha\,(1-\pi)} .

With \pi=0.10, power 0.80, \alpha=0.05: \text{PPV} = \frac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.045} = 0.64 — so 36% of significant findings are false, matching the “5 of 13” count once the 0.05\times90 = 4.5 false positives are rounded. As \pi\to 0 or power falls, \text{PPV}\to 0. This is identically the rare-disease Bayes formula with power playing the role of sensitivity and \alpha the role of the false-positive rate.
False discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg). Order the m p-values p_{(1)}\le\dots\le p_{(m)}; find the largest k with p_{(k)} \le \frac{k}{m}q and reject H_{(1)},\dots,H_{(k)}. This bounds the expected proportion of false discoveries at q — controlling a fraction rather than a probability, which is why it keeps more power than Bonferroni.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (every abuse here is a p-value misused), statistical power (underpowered studies feed truth inflation and low PPV), confidence intervals (estimation as the reform), and the base-rate fallacy (the skeleton of the PPV argument).
Leads to: the Bayesian sub-chapter (one response to p-value abuse is to reason about hypotheses’ probabilities directly), and to the tyranny of metrics in Part VI (what happens when “significant result” becomes a target to be gamed).
Closes: the Classical Toolkit. You now have the whole frequentist apparatus and a clear-eyed view of how it fails — the prerequisite for using it responsibly.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“List every mechanism of the replication crisis that Statistics Done Wrong describes, with the concrete example Reinhart uses for each.”

“Explain the multiple-comparisons problem, the dead-salmon study, and exactly how Bonferroni and the false discovery rate differ.”

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text: Every statistic you compute is a random variable in disguise: run the study again and you’d get a slightly different number. The sampling distribution is the set of numbers you could have gotten, and the Central Limit Theorem tells you that — for averages — that set is almost always a bell curve.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Why it matters
You run a survey, compute an average, and report it. But you only ran the survey once. Had a different 1,200 people answered the phone, your average would have come out a little different. So the real question behind every empirical claim is: how much would my answer have bounced around if chance had dealt a different sample?
That bounce is not a nuisance to apologize for — it is the thing inference is about. Margins of error, confidence intervals, p-values, standard errors: every one of them is a statement about a sampling distribution. Get this idea and the rest of statistics stops being a bag of formulas and becomes one question asked many ways.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Intuition
There are two different distributions that beginners constantly confuse, and keeping them apart is most of the battle. (Bruce et al. 2020)

A data distribution describes the individual values in one dataset — the heights of the people in your sample, say. Plot a histogram of your data and that’s what you see.
A sampling distribution describes how a summary of the data — the mean, a proportion, a treatment effect — would vary across many hypothetical datasets drawn the same way.

The leap is this: a statistic is a function of random data, so it is itself random. The sample mean \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i is a sum of random variables, and a sum of random variables has its own probability distribution. (Ross 2018) Grinstead & Snell make this concrete by simulation: keep drawing samples, compute the mean each time, and watch those means scatter into a shape of their own. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) In an experiment, Gerber & Green give the cleanest gloss — the sampling distribution is “the collection of estimates that could have been generated by every possible random assignment.” That is exactly what lets you ask whether your result was a lucky draw. (Gerber and Green 2012)

Watch it happen
The most convincing argument here is not a proof — it’s a picture. Start with a population that is emphatically not bell-shaped (a heavily skewed one), draw samples, and plot the distribution of the sample mean as the sample size grows.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# A strongly right-skewed "population": an exponential distribution.
# (Think: income, donation sizes, time-to-event — common in social data.)
pop_mean = 1.0
population = rng.exponential(scale=pop_mean, size=200_000)

sample_sizes = [2, 10, 30]
n_reps = 50_000

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11, 3.1))

# Panel 0: the raw population
axes[0].hist(population, bins=60, range=(0, 6), color="#9a6a2f", alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_title("Population\n(raw data, skewed)", fontsize=10)
axes[0].set_yticks([])

# Panels 1-3: sampling distribution of the mean at increasing n
for ax, n in zip(axes[1:], sample_sizes):
    means = population[rng.integers(0, population.size, size=(n_reps, n))].mean(axis=1)
    ax.hist(means, bins=60, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, density=True)
    ax.set_title(f"Mean of n = {n}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
    # overlay the Normal the CLT predicts: mean = pop_mean, sd = pop_sd / sqrt(n)
    pop_sd = population.std()
    xs = np.linspace(means.min(), means.max(), 200)
    se = pop_sd / np.sqrt(n)
    normal = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - pop_mean) / se) ** 2) / (se * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
    ax.plot(xs, normal, color="#b5446e", lw=1.8)

fig.suptitle("The sample mean forgets the shape it came from", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A skewed population (left) versus the sampling distribution of its mean at n = 2, 10, and 30. The raw data is lopsided, yet the averages march toward a bell.





The leftmost panel is the world as it actually is: lopsided. Yet by the time we average just 30 observations, the distribution of that average is almost perfectly Normal (the pink curve is the bell the theorem predicts). The sample mean forgets the shape it came from. That forgetting is the Central Limit Theorem.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
In plain language: if you add up or average enough independent random pieces, the result is approximately Normal — no matter what distribution the pieces came from. Francis Galton called it the “supreme law of unreason”: order emerging reliably out of individual chaos. (Grinstead and Snell 1997)
Stated carefully: let X_1, X_2, \dots be independent and identically distributed with finite mean \mu and finite variance \sigma^2. Then as n \to \infty, the standardized sample mean converges in distribution to a standard Normal:

Z_n \;=\; \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}(0, 1).

Two things deserve emphasis, because they are where the theorem is most often misremembered:

What it needs: independence, and a finite mean and variance. It does not need the data to be Normal — that’s the whole point. A version even allows the pieces to have different distributions, as long as no single one dominates the sum. (Ross 2018)
How fast: remarkably fast for symmetric data — a fair die’s average looks bell-shaped by n \approx 10. The famous “n \ge 30” is only a rule of thumb, and a bad one for heavily skewed data, which can need far more. (Bruce et al. 2020; Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Standard deviation vs standard error
This is the second great confusion, and it costs people dearly. (Reinhart 2015)

Standard deviation (\sigma) measures the spread of individual data points. How much do people differ from the average person? That’s the SD.
Standard error (SE) measures the spread of a statistic across hypothetical samples. It is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. How much would my estimate bounce around if I redid the study? That’s the SE. (Bruce et al. 2020)

They are linked by the single most useful formula in applied statistics:

\text{SE}(\bar{X}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.

The \sqrt{n} is doing quiet, brutal work. Because uncertainty shrinks with the square root of the sample size, to halve your standard error you must quadruple your sample. This is why precision is expensive, and why a poll of 1,200 is “good enough” while a poll of 4,800 is only twice as good. (Bruce et al. 2020)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Worked examples
An astronomer measures a distance. (Ross 2018) Atmospheric distortion and human error make any single telescope reading noisy. So the astronomer takes n independent readings and averages them. By the CLT, that average is approximately Normal around the true distance — and its spread, the SE, shrinks like \sigma/\sqrt{n}. More readings, sharper answer.
A pollster samples a nation. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) The CLT is why a sample of roughly 1,200 people pins down a national preference to about a \pm 3\% margin — and, strikingly, why that barely depends on whether the country has 100,000 or 100 million people. It’s the sample size that drives precision, not the population size.
Female village councils in India. (Gerber and Green 2012) When researchers asked whether reserving council-head seats for women shifted budget priorities, they used the sampling distribution of the estimated effect to ask: is the spending difference we observed bigger than what random assignment alone could have produced? That comparison is the inference.
The RAND Health Insurance Experiment. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Before trusting any treatment effect, the researchers used standard errors to check that groups assigned to different insurance plans were balanced at baseline — comparable starting points, within the bounds of chance.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Thinking your data should be Normal. The CLT is about the sampling distribution of a statistic, not your raw observations. Social-science data is routinely skewed and long-tailed (income, donations, durations). Forcing a Normal model onto raw data underestimates extreme “black swan” values. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Trusting n \ge 30 blindly. For strongly skewed data, t-tests and Normal approximations can be badly off even at moderate n — Reinhart’s example is charitable donations, where a few huge gifts wreck the approximation. (Reinhart 2015)
Eyeballing overlapping error bars. Two confidence intervals can overlap and the difference can still be significant. “Your eyeball is not a well-defined statistical procedure” — run the actual test. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating the bootstrap as a cure for small samples. Resampling your own data reveals the variability already present; it does not manufacture information you never collected. It frees you from assuming Normality, not from needing enough data. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The gambler’s fallacy and optional stopping. A run of heads does not make tails “due” — the Law of Large Numbers governs the long run, not short streaks. And peeking at your data repeatedly and stopping the moment p < 0.05 massively inflates false positives. (Grinstead and Snell 1997; Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the sample mean as a random variable; the formal CLT; the astronomer example.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — CLT via simulation; the polling example; the Law of Large Numbers and gambler’s fallacy.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — data vs sampling distribution; SD vs SE; the bootstrap; the “raw data isn’t Normal” warning.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — small-sample failures; overlapping error bars; optional-stopping inflation of false positives.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — the sampling distribution as “every possible random assignment”; the India village-council study.
Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless Econometrics — standard errors for baseline balance in the RAND experiment.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Mean and variance of the sample mean. Let X_1,\dots,X_n be i.i.d. with \mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu and \operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\sigma^2. Then by linearity of expectation,

\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu,

so the sample mean is unbiased. For the variance, independence makes the cross terms vanish:

\operatorname{Var}(\bar{X})
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2)
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.

Taking the square root gives the standard error, \text{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt{n}, and with it the \sqrt{n} rule.
The Central Limit Theorem (Lindeberg–Lévy form). Define the standardized mean

Z_n = \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu).

Then for every real z,

\lim_{n\to\infty} \Pr(Z_n \le z) = \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-t^2/2}\,dt .

Sketch of why. Work with the characteristic function \varphi(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{itZ_n}]. Writing Y_i=(X_i-\mu)/\sigma (mean 0, variance 1) and using independence,

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[\varphi_Y\!\left(\tfrac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)\right]^{n}.

A second-order Taylor expansion gives \varphi_Y(s) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2}s^2 + o(s^2), so

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[1 - \frac{t^2}{2n} + o\!\left(\tfrac{1}{n}\right)\right]^{n}
\;\longrightarrow\; e^{-t^2/2},

which is the characteristic function of the standard Normal. Lévy’s continuity theorem then delivers convergence in distribution. The finite-variance assumption is exactly what makes the quadratic term — and nothing higher — survive in the limit; when variance is infinite (heavy tails), the limit is a different, non-Normal stable law, which is the deep reason the CLT can fail.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Connections

Builds on: random variables, expectation and variance (the \sigma/\sqrt{n} standard error), the Normal distribution, and the Law of Large Numbers (which gives the destination the CLT then puts a shape around).
Leads to: confidence intervals and hypothesis testing, where the sampling distribution becomes the engine of inference.
Reappears in: the standard errors attached to every regression coefficient and every causal estimate.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“Walk me through Grinstead & Snell’s simulation argument for the Central Limit Theorem, step by step, as if I’d never seen it.”
“Give me three more examples from these sources where confusing the standard deviation with the standard error led to a wrong conclusion.”
“Explain the bootstrap as presented in Practical Statistics for Data Scientists, and be precise about what it can and cannot fix.”
“What exactly does Statistics Done Wrong say about optional stopping, and how badly does it inflate the false-positive rate?”
“When does the Central Limit Theorem fail or converge slowly? Find the conditions discussed across these sources.”

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text: This is where statistics meets the social world’s questions. The centre of gravity is regression: a tool for holding things constant, summarising relationships, and — with care — estimating effects. The undergraduate core: OLS, its assumptions, its inference, its honest diagnostics, and the limited-dependent-variable models for outcomes that aren’t continuous.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
The bridge to causality
Mastering ’Metrics frames regression as a way of making “other things equal.” That framing is the hinge between description and Causal Inference: a coefficient is only an effect if you have an argument for why the comparison is fair.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
Where this leads
Onward to Part IV · Predictive Modelling — the computational turn, where the goal shifts from explaining to predicting, and the same regression machinery is judged by a completely different standard.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text: The summit of the whole manual, and the centre of gravity of the source library. The question is deceptively simple — what is the effect of X on Y? — and answering it honestly requires a framework for imagining the world that didn’t happen, plus the design and computational tools built in every part before this one.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
The “Furious Five”
The applied core, as Angrist & Pischke frame it: randomised experiments, regression adjustment, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences. Each is a different argument for why a comparison is fair — a different way of approximating the experiment you wish you could have run.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
Where this leads
Down from the summit to Part VI · Doing Research in the World: having learned what a credible causal claim is, you learn what survives contact with measurement, incentives, and a client who needs an answer by Friday.

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
Machine learning is kept deliberately separate from the causal main track, and bridged to it only where it earns a place. The reason is the argument made in Predictive Modelling: ML is, by default, the predictive culture. Pretending its tools answer causal questions is one of the most common and costly confusions in applied work.
That said, the frontier of social science increasingly uses ML in service of causal goals — estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity, handling high-dimensional controls — and this track follows that bridge carefully.

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
The bridge back
Every entry here points back to the main spine: a prediction is not an effect, and a feature importance is not a cause. The ML track sharpens your tools without loosening your causal discipline.
